original_abstract:
Mapping gene regulatory networks typically requires vast amounts of transcriptomic data to resolve complex interactions. This reliance on large-scale datasets often hinders discoveries in fields where data are inherently scarce, such as rare diseases or conditions affecting clinically inaccessible tissues. In recent years, transfer learning has transformed fields like natural language processing and computer vision by utilizing deep learning models pretrained on massive, general datasets. These models can then be fine-tuned for a wide range of downstream applications using only limited, task-specific data. 

In this study, we present Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based deep learning model pretrained on a comprehensive corpus of approximately 30 million single-cell transcriptomes. Our framework is designed to enable high-resolution, context-specific predictions in network biology even when data are limited. We demonstrate that during the pretraining phase, Geneformer acquires a fundamental representation of network dynamics, effectively encoding cellular hierarchy and gene-gene relationships within its internal attention weights in an entirely unsupervised manner. This approach provides a robust foundation for identifying critical regulatory drivers and disease mechanisms across diverse biological contexts.
humanized_abstract:
Understanding gene regulatory networks requires vast quantities of transcriptomic data to maintain an adequate resolution. Due to this high data requirement, it is commonly the conditions lacking sufficient data that are left uncovered in research, specifically when it comes to rare diseases and/or hard to reach clinical specimens. In the few years, that the concept of transfer learning has been coined, we have seen numerous examples of great successes in the domain of natural language processing and computer vision through the utilization of deep learning frameworks and their pre-trained models, known as off-the-shelf deep learning models, which are initially pre-trained on a large, commonly available dataset that is rich in generic knowledge. Once such a basic, yet generic model is pre-trained and available, it only requires a tiny amount of data for fine tuning it for the specific task that is to be solved.

We introduce a deep learning model named Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based model pre-trained on a dataset of about 30 million single-cell gene expression profiles. Geneformer is capable of producing high-resolution, context-dependent predictions in network biology even when data are scarce. During pre-training, Geneformer learns a fundamental understanding of network behavior, and it learns to encode cellular hierarchy and pairwise gene interactions within its attention map in an unsupervised fashion. This knowledge is then leveraged to discover key regulatory genes and biology of diseases across different contexts.

We introduce a deep learning model named Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based model pre-trained on a dataset of about 30 million single-cell gene expression profiles. document.input:
Mapping gene regulatory networks typically requires vast amounts of transcriptomic data to resolve complex interactions. This reliance on large-scale datasets often hinders discoveries in fields where data are inherently scarce, such as rare diseases or conditions affecting clinically inaccessible tissues. In recent years, transfer learning has transformed fields like natural language processing and computer vision by utilizing deep learning models pretrained on massive, general datasets. These models can then be fine-tuned for a wide range of downstream applications using only limited, task-specific data. 

In this study, we present Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based deep learning model pretrained on a comprehensive corpus of approximately 30 million single-cell transcriptomes. We demonstrate that during the pretraining phase, Geneformer acquires a fundamental representation of network dynamics, effectively encoding cellular hierarchy and gene-gene relationships within its internal attention weights in an entirely unsupervised manner. This approach provides a robust foundation for identifying critical regulatory drivers and disease mechanisms across diverse biological contexts.
